coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn off-on detection of Co2+ and enrofloxacin by N, Cl-carbon dots prepared in deep eutectic solvent and study of their photocatalytic antibacterial activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tabaraki, Reza
Nazari, Fereshteh
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen, chloride doped carbon dots (N, Cl-CDs) were synthesized in deep eutectic solvent. TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, EDAX, UV–Vis and fluorescence were used for characterization. Quantum yield and average size of N, Cl-CDs were 38.75 % and 2–3 nm, respectively. N, Cl-CDs fluorescence was turn off by cobalt ion and then turn on gradually after addition of enrofloxacin. Linear dynamic range and detection limit (DL) for Co2+ and enrofloxacin were (0.1–70 µM and 30 nM) and (0.05–50 µM and 25 nM), respectively. Enrofloxacin was detected in blood serum and water samples with the recovery of 96–103 %. Finally, Antibacterial activity of the carbon dots was also investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Deep eutectic solvent
Carbon dots
Microwave
Fluorescence
Enrofloxacin
Antibacterial activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) absorption, and (C) fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs.
(A) FT-IR, (B) EDXS and (C) XPS spectra of the N, Cl-CDs.

(A) Relative fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs in the presence of various ions, (B) selectivity of N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system for enrofloxacin in the presence of 10 μM of drugs, (C) effect of pH on the relative florescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs in the presence of 10 μM enrofloxacin.
Stern-Volmer plot of Co2+ (A and B) and enrofloxacin (C and D).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of enrofloxacin and Co2+, (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N, Cl-CDs and N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system, (C) Zeta potential of N, Cl-CDs and N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system.

Antibacterial activity of N, Cl-CDs (28 mg/mL) against different bacteria: (A) Salmonella, (B) Klebsiella pneumoniae, (C) Escherichia coli, and (D) Staphylococcus aureus, (control: Tetracycline (T30 μg), Gentamicin(GM10 μg)).
Comparison of different methods for cobalt and enrofloxacin determination.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn off-on detection of Co2+ and enrofloxacin by N, Cl-carbon dots prepared in deep eutectic solvent and study of their photocatalytic antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen, chloride doped carbon dots (N, Cl-CDs) were synthesized in deep eutectic solvent. TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, EDAX, UV–Vis and fluorescence were used for characterization. Quantum yield and average size of N, Cl-CDs were 38.75 % and 2–3 nm, respectively. N, Cl-CDs fluorescence was turn off by cobalt ion and then turn on gradually after addition of enrofloxacin. Linear dynamic range and detection limit (DL) for Co2+ and enrofloxacin were (0.1–70 µM and 30 nM) and (0.05–50 µM and 25 nM), respectively. Enrofloxacin was detected in blood serum and water samples with the recovery of 96–103 %. Finally, Antibacterial activity of the carbon dots was also investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Enrofloxacin is a synthetic antibiotic which is mainly used in veterinary medicine. It has good inhibitory effect on the reproduction of different bacteria [1]. Overuse of enrofloxacin lead to accumulation in edible animals and bacterial resistance which has serious effects to human health after food consumption. So, enrofloxacin residual limits were set by European Union and Food and Drug Administration [2].

Analytical methods such as HPLC [3], mass spectrometry [4], LC-MS/MS [5] were developed for enrofloxacin measurement. Some limitations of such techniques are complex pre-treatment, time-consuming process, specialized and expensive instruments. Fluorescent carbon dots have properties such as low toxicity and cost, simplicity, and rapid response time. Doping of CDs by heteroatoms improves their quantum yields and fluorescence intensities, which is beneficial for sensors and bio-imaging [6]. It has been shown that doping of CDs by electron-withdrawing atoms such as nitrogen and chloride significantly improves fluorescent characteristics and these CDs have low toxicity in vitro and in vivo, good aqueous solubility, and favorable biocompatibility [7–9].

Until now, the significant antibacterial effects have been found in carbon nano-materials with advantages including non-toxicity, surface functionality and photo-stability [10]. Size, surface functional groups (amides and amines) and surface charge of carbon dots affect their antibacterial activity. Among different precursors for the synthesis of a wide variety carbon-based nano-materials, deep eutectic solvent (DES) are new candidates [11]. DESs show many important unique physicochemical properties such as high polarity, low volatility, diversity of precursors for its design, non-flammability, low cost, low toxicity and biodegradability [12]. In doped-CDs synthesis, these solvents are used as doping agent because they have diverse elements.

In this work, DES (choline chloride-glycerol) as nitrogen and chloride sources was used in microwave-assisted synthesis of N, Cl doped CDs. Selective and sensitive determination of Co2+ can be done by the N, Cl-CDs fluorescence quenching. After addition of the enrofloxacin to N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system, the CDs fluorescence was recovered. The N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system had excellent recognition for enrofloxacin. Simple preparation procedure, wide linear range, and acceptable detection limit are advantages to the new method. Finally, antibacterial activity of the N, Cl-CDs was investigated.

Choline chloride, glycerol, glucose, and phosphate buffer (0.1 M) were analytical grade and obtained from Merck (Dermasdat, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (BESTEC, EA 10, Germany), Varian Cary 300 Bio (Australia), Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Australia), MC30 Philips (Australia), X-ray spectroscopy (MIRA II TESCAN, Czech Republic), Vertex 70 FT-IR (Bruker, Germany), TEM (MCM120, Netherlands), and Zeta potential (Horiba, SZ100, Japan) were used for XPS, UV–Vis, Fluorescence, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS), FT-IR,TEM and Zeta potential study, respectively. Solution pH was measured by the pH-meter (Metrohm, model-780, Switzerland).

Briefly, DES was prepared from 4 mL of glycerol and 1.52 g choline chloride (molar ratio of 1:2) [13]. They were heated at 100 °C with constant stirring until 5 mL homogeneous liquid was obtained. Then, 0.1 g of glucose was dissolved in this solvent and heated in a microwave oven (350 W, 3 min). Resulting solid was dissolved (10 mL water), centrifuged (20 min at 12000 rpm) and filtered with 0.4 μm filter (final concentration: 28 mg/mL).

Effect of various ions on N, Cl-CDs fluorescence quenching was studied. 100 µL (N, Cl-CDs) and 10 μL of ion solution (10 mM) were added to 3 mL deionized water. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 540 nm (λex = 430 nm).

The N, Cl-CDs fluorescence can be turn off by Co2+. 100 µL N, Cl-CDs and 100 µL Co2+ (1 mM) were to the 3 mL phosphate buffer (pH = 6). After addition of the enrofloxacin (0.05 – 50 µM) to this solution, the N, Cl-CDs fluorescence turn on. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 540 nm (λex = 430 nm). All experiments were repeated three times (n = 3).
A spectrally integrated photon flux and absorption approach was used to quantum yield measurement [14]. Reference was quinine sulfate.

Human serum samples were diluted (50-fold) with phosphate buffer. Different concentration of enrofloxacin was mixed with 1 mL serum and then 2 mL acetonitrile was added to remove serum proteins and finally centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) [15].
Spiked tap samples were analyzed by the proposed method for enrofloxacin. All samples were filtered (0.45 μm), centrifuged (10000, 5 min) and adjusted to pH = 6 with phosphate buffer solution [5].

Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella (Gram-positive bacteria) and Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumonia (Gram-negative bacteria) were selected for antibacterial study. This property was measured by agar disc diffusion method. These bacteria (cultured in Luria Bertani broth, 37 °C, 24 h) were obtained from the Department of microbiology, Ilam University, Iran. 100 µL of each bacterium with adjusted to 0.5 McFarland was spread on the different plates. Various concentrations of N, Cl-CDs and control antibiotics (penicillin, ceftazidime) were kept in each plate and then incubated in the dark (37 °C, 24 h). Diameters of the inhibition zone were used for antibacterial activity measurement [16].

As shown Fig. 1 A, average size of the N, Cl-CDs was 2–3 nm. An absorption band centered on 280 nm (Fig. 1B) was assigned for the π → π* transitions for aromatic sp2 hybridization [16]. As shown, when excitation was at 430 nm, maximum emission was at 547 nm (Fig. 1C).

Fig. 2 A shows the N, Cl-CDs FT-IR. Strong bands at 3259, 2916, 1481, 1348, 1085, and 950 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of OH, CH, CO, CN, CO and CCl [17]. Based on EDXS spectrum (Fig. 2B), composition of the synthesized N, Cl-CDs were carbon (53.4 %), chloride (19.8 %), oxygen (14.5 %), and nitrogen (12.3 %). XPS results are shown in Fig. 2C. Four dominant peaks were assigned 284 eV (C 1s), 533 eV (O 1s), 400 eV (N 1s), and 198 eV (Cl). Application of fitting method on this spectrum approved the presence of the CC (284 eV), CO/CN (286 eV), COOH (288 eV), amine/amide N (399 eV), pyrrolic N (400 eV), COH (531 eV) and CO (532 eV) [12].

N, Cl-CDs had high fluorescent QY (38.75 %; reference: quinine sulfate). This high quantum yield was attributed to the doping of heteroatoms (N and Cl) in the CDs that introduce new surface states and thus enhances the photoluminescence performance.

Effect of 20 different ions at the same conditions (10 µM) on fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs was studied (Fig. 3 A). Only Co2+ ion can significantly quenched the N, Cl-CDs fluorescence. The N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ (50 µM) system showed high sensitivity toward enrofloxacin (Fig. 3B). Therefore, a turn off–on probe was used for the Co2+ and enrofloxacin detection.

Relative florescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system in pH range of 3–10 was studied in the presence of 10 μM enrofloxacin (Fig. 3C). Thus, pH = 6 was chosen as the optimum pH for the enrofloxacin determination, which could be ascribed to the electrostatic attraction between enrofloxacin and N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system [15].
Fluorescence recovery was fast after addition of enrofloxacin to N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system. Fast response time is an advantage of a good probe.

After addition of the Co2+ in the concentration range of 0.1–70 μM, the N, Cl-CDs fluorescence was linearly decreased (Fig. 4 A, B). A good relationship was observed (Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = 0.0321C Co2+ (µM) + 1.1372; R2 = 0.99; DL = 30 nM). New cobalt determination method was compared with previously reported methods [18–31] in Table 1 . After addition of the enrofloxacin in the concentration range of 0.05–50 µM, fluorescence of the N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system was linearly recovered (Fig. 4C, D). As shown in Fig. 5 B, Stern-Volmer equation was F0/F = 0.0246C enrofloxacin (µM) + 1.118, R2 = 0.99. Detection limit was 25 nM. Limit of detection was calculated according to 3sb/m, where sb is the standard deviation of blank signal (mean of blank fluorescence intensity = 800.00142, n = 5) and m is the slope. A comparison between new and previously reported methods [32–38] for enrofloxacin determination is also shown in Table 1. New method had wider linear range, acceptable detection limit, fast response time and simple and cheap synthesis.

The new method was tested by the determination of enrofloxacin in human serum and tap water. Recovery experiments were performed and results were listed in Table 2 . As shown, good recoveries (96.1 % to 103.43 %) and RSDs (1.4–5.4 %) were obtained.

Experiments were done to address whether the N, Cl-CDs without Co2+ can be applied for enrofloxacin detection or not. As shown in Fig. 5A, after addition of the enrofloxacin to the N, Cl-CDs (without Co2+), the intensity and shape of fluorescence spectrum had negligible change (λex = 430 nm). So, enrofloxacin had no interaction with the N, Cl-CDs.

UV–Vis spectra of the N, Cl-CDs and N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system are shown in Fig. 5B. Absorption band at 273 nm was declined after addition of Co2+ ions. The N, Cl-CDs surface functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic can interact with Co2+ ions through metal ligand interactions [17]. Therefore, Co2+ was liberated from N, Cl-CDs surfaces by enrofloxacin and the N, Cl-CDs fluorescence was recovered [15].

The zeta potential of the N, Cl-CDs was −10.8 mV at pH = 6 (Fig. 5C). This may be ascribed to the deprotonated functional groups of the N, Cl-CDs (including amino, carboxyl, and chloride). This potential changed to + 30 mV after the addition of Co2+ (Fig. 5C). Co2+ ions can easily form a complex with the N, Cl-CDs functional groups.

Dosage, light irradiation, size (>6 nm), charge and surface chemistry are dominant factors which affect the CDs toxicity. Effect of CDs charge on toxicity is neutral charge (less toxic) < negative charge < positive charge. Positive charges bind to negatively charged molecules such as nucleic acids and proteins and have a greater chance for opsonization [6,9]. Therefore, selection of appropriate nontoxic molecules for the CDs synthesis and surface functionalization should be considered. Carbon dots synthesized from choline chloride based deep eutectic solvents have low cost and low toxicity and choline chloride is a representative source of nitrogen and chloride atoms for doping. As mentioned, the zeta potential of the synthesized N, Cl-CDs in this study was −10.8 mV. Wang et al. [39] synthesized N-doped and N, Cl-doped carbon dots from dried shaddock peel and studied the effect of Cl doping. N, Cl-doped carbon dots had better quantum yield (four times) and smaller size than that of N-doped carbon dots. Then, these N, Cl-doped carbon dots were used for A549 and HeLa cell-imaging with optimal imaging effect and ultra-low cytotoxicity. Nafiujjaman et al. [40] synthesized N, Cl-GQDs by hydrothermal method from fructose for cancer cell imaging. In vitro study showed no toxicity effect in normal and cancer cells treated with N, Cl-GQDs.

Antibacterial activity of the N, Cl-CDs was investigated (Fig. 6 ). Effect of the N, Cl-CDs on Gram-positive bacterial was greater than Gram-negative bacteria. The N, Cl-CDs antibacterial efficiency was determined by their surface charge and size which can affect the attachment to the bacteria. The N, Cl-CDs with negative charges interact with positive-charge bacteria and lead to change in the surface charge balance of bacteria and inactivation [16].

The N, Cl-CDs were synthesized in deep eutectic solvent. After addition of Co2+ to the solution, the N, Cl-CDs fluorescence was quenched. The N, Cl-CDs/Co2+ system can be used directly as selective and eco-friendly turn on nano-probe for enrofloxacin. Therefore, this system was used as sensitive turn off–on probes for Co2+ and enrofloxacin with detection limits of 30 and 25 nM, respectively. These N, Cl-CDs had good fluorescence quantum yield (QY = 38.75 %) and stability. The precision and accuracy of the proposed probe were also acceptable. This fluorescent probe had a low LOD, a wide linearity range and a high sensitivity, selectivity and low cost.
Reza Tabaraki: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Fereshteh Nazari: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
